coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted ultrafast in-situ growth of N-doped carbon quantum dots on multiwalled carbon nanotubes as an efficient electrocatalyst for photovoltaics

coredata.dc:description: Multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) are at the forefront of metal-free electrocatalysts, however, the performance is still limited due to lack of functionality and dispersion. Coupling of MWCNTs with nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) can impart the required active sites and dispersion. For the purpose, NCQDs are generally attached to MWCNTs by multistep processing, such as NCQDs synthesis, followed by their complex purification, surface activation, and crosslinking with MWCNT. The scalability of such a multistep process is limited, which is addressed by direct microwave-assisted growth of NCQDs on MWCNT. The concentration of reactants of NCQDs synthesis was optimized (with respect to MWCNTs), to achieve controlled direct growth of NCQDs on MWCNTs. The proposed strategy significantly reduced time and energy consumption, along with providing an overlapped interface for the fast charge transfer. Moreover, NCQDs' growth effectively modulated the surface reactivity and internal band structure of the MWCNTs. In response, dye-sensitized solar cells employing NCQDs modified MWCNT as a counter electrode showed 50% higher photovoltaic performance as compared to bare MWCNTs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of rapid synthesis NCQDs grown MWCNTs, where, mild oxidation generates active sites for the growth. By exposing the functional MWCNTs to microwaves in the presence of urea and citric acid, NCQDs are grown on their surface. The inset table compares the short time, lower energy consumption, and a high production yield of microwave-assisted synthesis.

Schematic representation of NCQDs growth stages. TEM analysis of structural variations (10 nm scale bar). Stage 1 highlighting pristine MWCNT, Stage 2 highlighting Acid modified MWCNT and Stage 3 is showing NCQDs@MWCNT.
TEM analysis (scale bar 10 nm) of NCQDs growth on (a) outer sidewalls, (b) edge, and (c) inner-walls (d) A high-resolution interface of NCQDs@MWCNT, and (e) associated FFT image.
Characterization of bare MWCNT and NCQDs@MWCNT using (a,b,c,d) SEM, (e) WA-XRD, (f) FTIR, and (g) XPS survey spectrum. Deconvoluted XPS survey spectrum for (h,i) C 1S and (j) N 1S.

Cyclic voltammogram of MWCNT (a) at different developmental stages, (b) loaded with different concentrations of NCQDs, highlighting the increase in EPP due to the overloading of NCQDs. (c) SEM image of high concentration loading of NCQDs on MWCNT. (d) Cyclic voltammetry of bare FTO-glass and NCQDs film coated on FTO glass.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms of the composite structure at different scan rates. Electrochemical cyclic stability of the electrode by (b) 200 scan cycles of the composite structure (35 mV/sec scan rate) with inset showing uniform coating formed by tape casting and (c) 50 cycles of Pt electrode (10 mV/sec scan rate).
Comparison of bare MWCNT and NCQDs@MWCNT by (a) EIS plot and (b) J-V curve.

Comparison of structural parameters after NCQDs growth using (a) UV–visible spectroscopy, (b) Raman spectroscopy, and (a) Cyclic voltammetry to measure the HOMO-LUMO level using Ferrocene as an external reference. (d) Variation in electronic structure by NCQDs growth.
Comparison of recent N-doped MWCNTs counter electrodes for DSSC.

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted ultrafast in-situ growth of N-doped carbon quantum dots on multiwalled carbon nanotubes as an efficient electrocatalyst for photovoltaics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Citric acid and urea were carbonized to N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) by 10 min exposure to microwaves, on the surface of MWCNTs. NCQDs modified MWCNT showed 50% higher photovoltaic performance.

Multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) are at the forefront of metal-free electrocatalysts, however, the performance is still limited due to lack of functionality and dispersion. Coupling of MWCNTs with nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) can impart the required active sites and dispersion. For the purpose, NCQDs are generally attached to MWCNTs by multistep processing, such as NCQDs synthesis, followed by their complex purification, surface activation, and crosslinking with MWCNT. The scalability of such a multistep process is limited, which is addressed by direct microwave-assisted growth of NCQDs on MWCNT. The concentration of reactants of NCQDs synthesis was optimized (with respect to MWCNTs), to achieve controlled direct growth of NCQDs on MWCNTs. The proposed strategy significantly reduced time and energy consumption, along with providing an overlapped interface for the fast charge transfer. Moreover, NCQDs' growth effectively modulated the surface reactivity and internal band structure of the MWCNTs. In response, dye-sensitized solar cells employing NCQDs modified MWCNT as a counter electrode showed 50% higher photovoltaic performance as compared to bare MWCNTs.

ce:section.ce:para: The greatest challenge facing humanity is the creation of sustainable energy sources to meet the increasing demand. To fulfill this global appetite for energy, photovoltaic energy has emerged as a sustainable alternative solution to depleting coal and oil resources. In this regard, third-generation solar cells: specifically dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) hold great potential, as they offer a comparative photovoltaic performance, with a facile fabrication. Metal-free earth-abundant materials based electrocatalysts for DSSC and energy storage devices are the center of research, which deliver suitable electrocatalytic activity with low cost and high stability. The efficient electrocatalytic properties of carbon materials, particularly multiwalled carbon nano-tubes hold a great promise in this regard, thanks to their high chemical stability, low cost, and considerable electrocatalytic activity [1,2]. Importantly, the electrocatalytic activity of MWCNTs is highly dependent on their degree of dispersion, heteroatom doping, and surface functionality. Specifically, hetero atom (nitrogen) doped MWCNTs are currently emerging as a frontier for efficient electrocatalysis. The conventional route for the synthesis of N-doped MWCNTs requires harsh chemical nitridation for long processing time and high temperatures. In contrast, we demonstrated a rapid (10 min) and low energy consuming modification of MWCNTs by in-situ growth of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) using microwaves. Table 1 summarises the comparison of our proposed method with existing recent N-doped carbon materials for the counter electrode of DSSCs.

MWCNTs consist of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms arranged in a tubular fashion, which provides high electrical conductivity, corrosion resistance, and thermal stability. Typically, MWCNTs have smooth sp2 hybridized graphitic planes, which provide an unimpeded flow of electrons. Conversely, such a smooth surface lacks in electrocatalytic activity, due to the high aggregation and limited active sites available for the charge transfer reaction. In order to impart the electrocatalytic activity, the surface of MWCNTs is effectively modified by surface oxidation, heteroatom doping, and surface passivation with surfactants [3–9]. By the given surface modifications, the conductivity of the MWCNTs network is compromised because conducting planes are damaged by oxidation. Similarly, polymer surfactants/binders adsorb on the surface of MWCNTs, thus the direct interconnections of MWCNTs are drastically hampered. In contrast, high conductivity is a key requirement in counter electrode applications; to avoid this issue, MWCNTs are coupled with other active materials. Amongst the other carbon materials, NCQDs are the ideal candidate for surface decoration of MWCNTs, with competing advantages of edge-abundant surface, stability, low cost, and facile synthesis. In addition, the light-harvesting capability of NCQDs can be used for multimodal applications, for instance, photocatalysis, photothermal energy conversion etc [10].

Thanks to the small size, NCQDs impart high surface functionality, high surface area, and low charge transfer resistance [5]. In order to get benefit from the attractive attributes of NCQDs, a conductive nano-support is mandatory to provide sufficient surface area to accommodate NCQDs without self-aggregation [3]. NCQDs (without nano-substrate) lose the performance by their self-aggregation; because of the limited exposed surface area, poor conductivity of aggregates, and complex electrolyte flow dynamics through NCQDs aggregates. Essentially, by using MWCNTs as support, the intrinsic limitations of MWCNTs can also be addressed, for instance, limited active sites and functionality. For the purpose, NCQDs are crosslinked with MWCNTs by various methods, such as adsorption, thermal crosslinking, catalyst mediated coupling, electrophoretic deposition, and electro-grafting [18–24]. These coupling methods consume a long time in purification and separation of NCQDs; moreover, catalyst-assisted surface activation of MWCNTs or NCQDs is an additional time and material consuming step. Time and energy consumption in such multistep coupling processes limit the scalability of post-coupled heterostructures, as synthesis time exceeds 1 to 7 days [19–24]. To reduce the synthesis time, in-situ controlled growth of NCQDs is a possible solution. In-situ growth of NCQDs on MWCNTs has not been extensively explored, where few of the studies on hydrothermal growth are reported [25]. Despite these efforts, the synthesis time extends more than 24 h. To reduce the synthesis time to a few minutes, we explored the potential for microwave-assisted in-situ grown NCQDs for the first time.

Microwave-assisted synthesis of NCQDs involves fast carbonization of reactants, yielding a defect rich morphology. By microwave exposure, the carbon source is initially condensed to flouropores (fluorescent organic molecules), which is then carbonized to carbon quantum dot. Short synthesis time, negligible content of byproducts, and abundant surface defects of microwave synthesized NCQDs are the attractive attributes to be utilized for the design of electrocatalyst. With this motivation, we utilized microwave-assisted NCQDs growth on MWCNTs for a promising electrocatalyst of DSSC. We optimized the concentration of growth precursor of NCQDs, to achieve a uniform and controlled growth on the MWCNT substrate. Indeed, an overlapped interface between NCQDs and MWCNT was formed by in-situ growth, which enables the rapid mutual charge transfer. In turn, the modified structure showed a 50% higher photovoltaic performance of DSSCs, as compared to bare MWCNTs. The enhanced performance of NCQDs@MWCNT was attributed to the effective tuning of internal band structure and surface reactivity. Our suggested method can be further extended for the surface modification of other catalyst systems, thanks to its competing advantages of facile and scalable production.

MWCNTs with a carbon content of ≥ 98%, length of 3–6 µm, and a diameter of 10 nm were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Nitric acid (70%, Sigma Aldrich) was used for the surface modification of MWCNTs. Other chemicals used for the growth of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs), including anhydrous citric acid (99%) and urea powder were provided by Daejung Chemicals, Korea. Other chemicals used for fabrication of DSSC were provided by Dyesol Australia, which includes TiO2, N719 dye, chloroplatinic acid, and Surlyn (60 µm). Chemicals required for electrolyte (I2, KI, LiIO4) and binding agent carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) (sodium salt of MW 250,000 g) were bought from Sigma Aldrich. Deionized water and acid-washed glassware were used throughout the experiment.

A schematic illustration of the experimental method is given in Fig. 1 . MWCNT powder (1 g) was refluxed in nitric acid (100 ml) for 30 min at 80 °C, and after filtration, functionalized MWCNT powder was collected by freeze-drying. Functional MWCNTs were dispersed in 10 ml of water by sonication. Afterward, citric acid (0.3 g) and urea (0.6 g) were dissolved in the functional MWCNTs dispersion by magnetic stirring. The above ratio of citric acid and urea was chosen as optimized previously by others to achieve high nitrogen doping [26]. However, here we optimized the ratio of precursors and MWCNTs (1 g); by using different concentrations of citric acid and urea. For testing the effect of high concentrations, 0.4 g citric acid and 0.8 g of urea were used for NCQDs synthesis. Whereas for testing lower content of NCQDs, we used 0.2 g and 0.4 g of citric acid and urea, respectively. A homogeneously mixed solution was then subjected to microwave treatment at 700 W, for 10 min. A commercial microwave oven manufactured by Daewoo Korea was used for the experiment. The obtained dry powder after microwave treatment was again washed with deionized water to remove unattached NCQDs and unreacted traces of chemicals. After washing, the NCQDs modified MWCNT heterostructure was dried in a vacuum oven at 70 °C, which was labeled as NCQDs@MWCNT. Finally, the dried powder was coated on Fluorine-doped Tin oxide (FTO) glass, to be used as a counter electrode for DSSC, using the method reported previously [27]. Briefly, for 1 g of carbon materials, 10 ml water and 0.07 g of CMC were thoroughly mixed using vigorous magnetic stirring to make a paste-like solution [28]. The resultant thick carbon paste can be coated on the larger area by simple tape casting/screen printing. The printed binder was partially carbonized by thermal treatment to restore conductivity in the carbonaceous network [27]. Also, the solid-state membrane-type electrolyte and separator further ensure that there is no peeling off or short circuit from the carbon electrode, as optimized in our previous work [27]. To elucidate the significance of the NCQDs growth, the photovoltaic performance of NCQDs@MWCNTs was compared to the pristine bare MWCNTs. The solar cell fabrication and characterization methods are given in supporting information.

A facile strategy was designed for the in-situ growth of NCQDs on the surface of MWCNTs, as depicted schematically in Fig. 1. Carbon nanotubes possess highly crystalline smooth tubular basal planes, which account for high conductivity in the MWCNT networks. However, such smooth graphitic planes lack catalytic activity due to limited active sites for the red/ox reaction. Such lower surface functionality of MWCNTs is also inappropriate for the heterogeneous growth of other active materials. Therefore, the required active sites for the growth of NCQDs were generated by mild nitric acid treatment. The acid treatment derivatized the outer sidewalls and edges of MWCNTs, which introduces additional oxygenated functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and ketones [29]. Such functional groups are formed due to the partial surface oxidation of the MWCNT by the oxidizing nature of the acid. These newly formed functional groups serve as an active site for the heterogeneous growth of NCQDs. Hydrophilic-hydrophilic interactions between the acid-modified MWCNT surface functional groups and constituent active species of NCQDs facilitate the formation of NCQDs nuclei on the surface of MWCNTs [20,30]. The oxidized MWCNTs hence serve as a template for the in-situ growth of carbon quantum dots. It is important to note that the acid treatment was carried out for a very short time here, so the effect of surface functionalization was almost negligible in catalytic activity, unlike previous works were the acid treatment time is more than 24 h.

The microwave-assisted growth on the surface of MWCNTs can be explained by the condensation and carbonization of citric acid and urea in four steps, Fig. S1(a), as reported previously [31,32]. The energy required for this reaction is carried by the microwaves. The precursors absorb the electromagnetic energy carried by microwaves and change it to heat energy (Step 1, Fig. S1a). This transformation of energy occurs either through the resonance or relaxation of materials. By the heat generated, the citric acid and urea are initially pyrolyzed to an intermediate carbonization stage: fluorophores (partially carbonized colorful molecules) [31,32]. The formation of fluorophores and NCQDs is spontaneous, hence exact reaction and structure are not reported yet. However, the approximated structure of fluorophore is depicted in Fig. S1, Step 2, which is formed by the condensation of citric acid and urea. Upon extending the microwave exposure time, fluorophores are further carbonized into a condensed intermediate particle form (Fig. S1, Step 3). Finally, the condensation polymerization and carbonization of intermediate particles proceeds, which results in the formation of NCQDs (Fig. S1, Step 4) [31–33]. Suitable carbonization of both precursors was achieved by using the given microwave energy, as optimized in the previous report [26]. Synthesis conditions define the crystallinity and band structure of quantum dots, as the degree of carbonization is determined by the energy carried by microwaves. The carbonization requires minimum microwave energy of 500 W, below which the pyrolysis of citric acid and urea cannot occur [33]. Similarly, carbonization at lower energies (500–600 W) requires a longer time for the pyrolysis, along with yielding highly amorphous and polymer carbon dots. Highly insulating nature of such an amorphous structure increases their charge transfer resistance, which is not suitable for electrocatalysis. By the given (700 W) energy conditions, the crystalline core and partially oxidized surface were achieved, which combines both the interesting features of fast charge transfer and superior surface functionality. Conventionally, microwaves assisted carbonization/pyrolysis uses the maximum microwave energy of around 800 W [33]. The pyrolysis is not uniform throughout the solution when higher power of microwaves (>1000 W) is used, hence the uniform size control is a problem at higher energies. The presence of functional MWCNTs during this carbonization process leads to the in-situ growth of NCQDs on the surface of MWCNTs, as demonstrated in Fig. 1.

The open vessel type microwave reactor (normal pressure) was used in the experiment so that evaporation can be possible during the microwave treatment. Initially, the water used as a solvent is evaporated by the heating effect of microwaves and afterward, further heating starts the growth process. Citric acid mainly serves as a carbon source, which is readily susceptible to carbonization at low temperatures. On the other hand, urea serves as the nitrogen source, which is pyrolyzed to release the active nitrogenous species for doping in the carbon skeleton of NCQDs (as shown in Fig. S1(a)). The water molecules formed as a result of condensation reaction are evaporated out during the reaction. Interestingly, this proposed ultrafast one-step fabrication of heterogeneous composite structure eliminates the complex purification steps of NCQDs and catalyst assisted surface modifications of MWCNTs. As the microwave pyrolysis for NCQDs synthesis yields negligible content of byproducts, so additional purification by dialysis tube is essentially avoided. The carbonization time for the microwave-assisted synthesis is 10 min, which is dramatically lower than conventionally used techniques. Moreover, compared to thermal pyrolysis and hydrothermal growth of NCQDs, microwaves assisted synthesis consumes 35 times lower energy, as summarized in the inset table of Fig. 1. The energy and time consumption is calculated by the same method as reported previously [34].

Structural changes during the growth and growth process of NCQDs on MWCNTs were analyzed at high resolutions using TEM, as shown in Fig. 2 . NCQDs synthesized via microwave-assisted carbonization (homogeneously grown) are shown in Fig. S1(b), with a size distribution of 2–10 nm (Fig. S1(c)). The second building block for the proposed catalyst design: pristine MWCNTs showed a uniform tubular structure, as shown in stage 1 of Fig. 2. The uniform surface is due to continuous conjugated CCCC bonds, as depicted in the high-resolution inset of bare MWCNTs. On the other hand, the aforementioned acid modification yielded additional defects on the surface of functionalized MWCNT, as confirmed by the slightly bamboo-like matrix surface of acid-treated MWCNTs (Fig. 2; stage 2). The acid treatment breaks the existing (CCC) bonds, and additional oxygenated functional groups are added in the carbon skeleton. Formation of the new oxygenated surface bonds imparts surface defects and wrinkles on the sidewalls, as the bond length of new oxidized bonds is greater than existing conjugated bonds, as shown schematically in high resolution of functional MWCNTs. The functional surface of modified MWCNTs can adsorb citric acid and urea species on the surface, which are carbonized on the surface of MWCNTs upon microwaves treatment. Direct surface growth of NCQDs is represented schematically in stage 3 of Fig. 2. The growth process is driven by the carbonization of reactants starts from the surface of functional MWCNT, as explained above (Fig. S1(a)).

TEM analysis shows the growth of NCQDs on outer sidewalls, edges, and inner walls as shown in Fig. 3 (a, b, and c), respectively. Such spatial distribution of growth confirms that acid treatment functionalizes the sidewalls, edges, and inner sidewalls of MWCNTs. Previously, NCQDs and other functional materials are filled inside and on the surface of the MWCNT by using complex techniques, such as by applying biased voltage. However, the versatility of the microwaves-assisted growth method allows the facile controlled growth of NCQDs on inner and outer walls. The quantum dots grown on edges and side walls showed a circular to random spontaneous shape, as shown in Fig. S2. The low-resolution image (Fig. S2(a)) shows the overall amount of NCQDs grown on MWCNTs. The size distribution of NCQDs directly grown on MWCNTs is given in Fig. S2(b). The slightly increased size and elongated shape were observed for the NCQDs grown on MWCNTs, compared to homogeneously grown NCQDs. The NCQDs formed on the outer surface showed a random circular shape, as observed in Fig. S2(c,d). However, NCQDs formed in the inner tubular cavities of MWCNTs showed a slightly oval shape, due to the restriction of growth in all directions (Fig. S3). Similarly, these elongated shaped NCQDs were also observed on some of the outer boundaries (Fig. S4), which validates the diffusion-controlled growth of NCQDs, by using our proposed concentration of reactants. The MWCNTs network passivates the access of constituent species during the growth process. Hence, under the limited incoming flux of monomer species, the pseudo-one-dimensional growth of one crystal face occurs, as observed in previous reports [35]. High-resolution analysis of NCQDs@MWCNT structure revealed well-defined crystal fringes for the grown NCQDs and MWCNTs, as clear from Fig. 3(d). The formation of an overlapped interface between MWCNT and NCQDs confirms that the in-situ growth of NCQDs starts from the defects sites, as highlighted in Fig. S4. In contrast, catalyst assisted attachment of NCQDs shows isolated random lattice fringes for NCQD and MWCNT [24]. The structure of individual NCQD shows crystalline and well-defined atomic arrangements, which can be differentiated from the crystal fringes of MWCNTs, as shown in Fig. S4. These crystalline regions are responsible for the central bright spot and other radial bright spots formed in the FFT image of NCQDs@MWCNT (Fig. 3(e)). Whereas the hazy portions formed in the FFT image are formed as a result of defects in MWCNTs and NCQDs. The TEM and FFT pattern of NCQDs@MWCNT sows that our proposed strategy integrates suitable defects (serve as an active site) and a crystalline conjugated system (assist charge flow).

The surface morphology of in-situ grown NCQDs@MWCNTs and bare MWCNTs coatings was further investigated by FE-SEM, as shown in Fig. 4 (a–d). Bare MWCNTs displayed a high degree of aggregation, as observed at different resolutions (Fig. 4(a) and inset). In comparison, NCQDs@MWCNTs revealed aggregates-free uniform film, as shown in Fig. 4(b) and its inset. Primarily, the aggregated clusters are formed in bare MWCNTs coatings because of their low surface functionality; hence, their colloidal dispersion is highly prone to micro-scale agglomerations. Unwanted agglomerated MWCNTs structure has poor electrolyte diffusion and complex charge flow dynamics in DSSC electrodes. Such unwanted agglomerations were radically avoided by NCQDs passivation on MWCNT’s surface, as clear from Fig. 4(c and d). The surface of NCQDs has excessive oxygenated functional groups, such as OH, CO, O. The electronegativity of such surface functional groups induce a strong electrostatic negative charge, which facilitates the superior dispersion of NCQDs@MWCNT structure. Notably, the dimensions of NCQDs were slightly different from the TEM measurements, which is due to excessive Pt coating applied before FE-SEM analysis. Our proposed, NCQDs assisted stabilization of MWCNTs is a suitable alternative to polymeric ligands assisted dispersion of MWCNTs: as polymer layer covers the MWCNTs surface, which hampers the interactions with electrolyte, along with lowering the mutual conductive connections [4].

The crystal structure of the NCQDs modified heterostructure was analyzed using wide-angle X-ray diffraction (WA-XRD). WA-XRD (Fig. 4 (e)) revealed the characteristic (002) graphitic plane peak of pure NCQDs, centered at 23.7°, as observed by others [36,37]. Compared to the plane spacing of the (002) graphitic plane of natural graphite, increased plane spacing was observed due to excessive surface functional groups on the surface of NCQDs [36]. Mutual repulsive forces between the electronegative surface groups induced an increase in the interlayer spacing of NCQDs [36]. On the other hand, the core structure revealed a highly compact structure, causing the NCQDs peak to extend to 40°. In addition to the characteristic peak of NCQDs, the NCQDs@MWCNT composite structure showed an additional overlapped peak centered at 26.6° due to MWCNTs. This dominant characteristic peak of MWCNT can be indexed to the (002) graphitic plane of MWCNTs. Compared to the MWCNT peak, the NCQDs peak is broad and has much lower intensity; which is due to its relatively smaller size (than MWCNT). Similar to (002) graphitic plane, comparatively lower-intensity of (100) and (004) planes was observed in NCQDs (at an angle of 43.6° and 54.7°) as shown in the inset of Fig. 4(e). Typically, this effect is observed due to the ultrasmall size of NCQDs with excessive surface defects.

The changes in surface functional groups, chemical structure, and atomic composition caused by the growth of NCQDs were analyzed by FTIR and XPS. FTIR results (Fig. 4(f)) confirmed a higher content of functional groups after the growth of NCQDs. Mainly, oxygenated and nitrogenated surface groups were observed in the modified heterostructure, as labeled in Fig. 4(f). XPS results are also analogous to the oxygenated surface functional groups driven growth of NCQDs on MWCNTs. Pristine MWCNTs possess 99% carbon content, whereas acid treatment and NCQDs growth generated the additional functional groups on the carbon skeleton, as clear from the XPS survey of functional MWCNT and NCQDs@MWCNT in Fig. 4(g). The high-resolution C1s peak of acid-modified functional MWCNTs displays a conspicuous skewness, caused by the carboxylic acids, carbonyl, and carboxylate-like surface oxygen moieties (labeled in Fig. 4(h)). Compared to the functional MWCNTs, the content of the surface functional groups is significantly increased after the growth of NCQDs, as shown in Fig. 4(i). The deconvoluted C1s peak of the composite structure can be fitted into four component peaks, centered at a binding energy of 283.7, 285.3, 286.8, and 287.7 eV binding energy, which corresponds to sp2 hybridized (CC) carbon, CNO, CO, and OCO bonds [38,39]. The core level hybridized carbon structure mainly arises from the graphitic MWCNTs; however, high content of the aminated and oxygenated surface groups are associated with the grown NCQDs. Due to small size, NCQDs can accommodate an exceptionally high concentration of surface functional groups, which essentially serves as an active site for electrochemical charge transfer reactions [40].

The role of doped nitrogen is critical in imparting the superior performance to the NCQDs@MWCNT structure. Therefore, nitrogen-doped carbon structures have been the most widely studied [5,6,41]. Thanks to the compatible atomic radius and auxochromic nature of doped nitrogen atoms, the required surface properties were achieved without compromising the conductivity of the carbon skeleton [5,6]. Overall 7% nitrogen was incorporated in the composite structure by the NCQDs growth, as shown in Fig. 4(g). The high-resolution spectrum of doped nitrogen (Fig. 4(j)) shows that doped nitrogen exists in pyridinic (CNC), pyrrolic (CNC), and graphitic (C3-N) states. The notable content of graphitic nitrogen is essentially doped by the proposed facile bottom-up method whereas, in previous top-down/surface modification techniques, the only surface passivated nitrogen is observed. Essentially, the aromatic-nitrogen (Pyridinic N) embedded in the graphitic core structure of NCQDs increases the density of electrons near the Fermi level, facilitating the low energy n-π* transitions of electrons, through NCQDs. Hence, the electronic band structure is substantially modified by the proposed bottom-up nitrogen doping, together with abundant surface attached nitrogen. The role of surface aminated groups is vital in the dispersion stability and low energy charge transfer in the electrocatalytic reactions, due to the electron donor nature of surface nitrogen [42]. The above characterizations evidence that by the suitable choice of materials, our proposed method incorporates extraordinary surface functionality, without compromising the conductivity of carbon structure.

The efficacy of NCQDs growth in enhancing the electrocatalytic activity of MWCNTs was tested by using cyclic voltammetry in iodide-based dummy electrolyte. In general, an efficient electrocatalyst assists in charge transfer reaction, which boosts the regeneration of reduced species in the electrolyte. Regenerated electrolyte species (e.g. I2) then transfer an electron to satisfy the holes of the dye molecules in photoanode. Here, the presence of excessive reduced (I2) species in the electrolyte shuttle assures the lower charge recombination in the excited dye molecules. As a result, the reduction capability and electrocatalytic performance of the electrocatalyst affect the performance of DSSC. Cyclic voltammetry is a powerful technique commonly employed to evaluate the electrocatalytic performance of the catalyst materials. The cyclic voltammograms obtained for the carbon electrodes are given in Fig. 5 (a), where higher current densities, along with a lower peak to peak potential (EPP) reflect the stronger potency of reduction. Enhanced electrocatalytic performance of the composite structure is obvious from the two folds higher current densities, specifically the negative peak intensity. Moreover, the NCQDs growth lowered the EPP to 250 mV whereas the bare MWCNTs have a high EPP of 600 mV, as highlighted in Fig. 5(a). Our catalyst system achieved lower EPP compared to recently reported nitrogen doped carbon nanotubes systems [4]. The acid-treated functional MWCNT showed slightly lower EPP, however, the current density remained unchanged, as clear from Fig. 5(a). The nominal increase in electrocatalytic activity by acid treatment can be ascribed to a short time of acid treatment, whereas long-time treatments can cause a further increase in current density. Categorically, modification of the bare MWCNTs structure by using facile NCQDs growth is an effective tool to improve electrocatalytic activity by more than twofold. Similarly, the electrocatalytic performance of the composite structure was superior to the conventionally used Pt electrode, as shown in Fig. S5. Mainly the composite electrode showed a slightly higher EPP, however, the current density was significantly higher than the Pt electrode. A higher current density of carbon materials is commonly observed due to higher surface area in carbon nano-network. In contrast, the EPP of Pt is lower than the composite structure, by virtue of which Pt electrode can complete the electrocatalytic cycle at faster speeds (i.e. at lower over-potential). Based on suitable electrocatalytic activity, the composite structure can be considered suitable for the Pt free DSSC.

The enhanced electrocatalytic activity of our proposed heterostructure is due to the strong interaction between the newly formed NCQDs surface and the electrolyte. Functional groups enriched surface of NCQDs can attract an exceptional amount of tri-iodide species, which are readily reduced by their highly reactive surface. The surface reactivity of NCQDs is explained on the basis of slightly higher electronegativity of the doped nitrogen/surface oxygen as compared to carbon. Because of this property, the doped structure has a partially negative and positive charge in the nitrogenated/oxygenated and carbon atom regions, respectively. Such a partially charged structure strongly attract the oppositely charged electrolyte species, thanks to mutual electrostatic interactions [43]. Moreover, the high surface area provided by the small size of NCQDs can accommodate the excessive content of electrolyte, causing a higher concentration of iodide species to be reduced by the NCQDs@MWCNT structure. Owing to the reduction of abundant electrophilic species, the composite structure revealed higher electrocatalytic current densities. The lower EPP of the composite structure is directly associated with the low energy electron transitions in the NCQDs decorated structure. In essence, the lone pair electrons added from nitrogen doped in NCQDs increases the density of electrons in the p-π conjugating electron cloud of MWCNTs. The modulation of the electrons density in the NCQDs modified structure enhances the conductivity and facilitates the charge transfer reactions at low potential [44]. Indeed, the rapid growth of NCQDs effectively modifies the electronic structure and interface, suitably required for the highly active catalyst materials.

In order to elucidate the role of grown NCQDs and to optimize the concentration of NCQDs inclusion, we compared the electrocatalytic performance of the NCQDs@MWCNT with varying NCQDs content, as given in Fig. 5(b). By using the aforementioned optimized concentration of citric acid and urea, comparatively improved performance was achieved, whereas lower catalytic activity was observed at both lower and higher concentrations. This can be regarded as the formation of monodispersed and uniformly distributed NCQDs in the MWCNTs network while using optimized concentration. In contrast, by using lower concentrations, the content of NCQDs formed is much lower, which renders lower catalytic activity. Similarly, at a higher concentration of citric acid (0.4 g) and urea (0.8 g), the large-sized aggregated NCQDs lumps are formed, as highlighted in Fig. 5(c). These agglomerates of NCQDs hinder the electrolyte diffusion and interconnections of MWCNTs, which drastically increase the resistance of such a composite structure. Thus, with an optimized concentration of citric acid (0.3 g) and urea (0.6 g), adequate size of NCQDs was grown, which boosts the catalytic performance. The direct growth and MWCNT support play a critical role in imparting the superior performance to the NCQDs, as pure NCQDs film shows almost negligible activity 5(d). High charge transfer resistance of pure NGQDs film is a problem in their application in pure form, hence bare FTO like electrocatalytic activity was observed in pure NCQDs film. Apart from the charge transport obstacle, the aggregation-induced quenching of NCQDs performance is a drawback that limits their performance in pure form. However, the self-aggregation of NCQDs can be radically avoided by the high surface area provided by the MWCNT nano-network. Moreover, the conductivity of MWCNTs provides a multidimensional framework for charge transport.

In addition to high electrocatalytic activity, the electrochemical stability of the material is a critical consideration to assure the long lifetime of DSSC. Therefore, the electrochemical stability of the proposed structure was analyzed at different scan rates and repeated electrocatalytic cycles, as given in Fig. 6 . A linear increase in scan rate caused a proportionate increase in the electrocatalytic current density, along with an increase in EPP (Fig. S6(a)). Similar characteristics have been commonly observed in the porous carbon nanonetworks, as indicated by other researchers [41–43]. The shape of the cyclic voltammogram was retained even at fast scan speeds, which proves that the composite structure has suitable porosity. Indeed, modification of MWCNTs with NCQDs combines conductivity and porosity in the carbon network, which enables the retention of electrocatalytic activity at high scan speeds. Additionally, the electrochemical stability of the electrode was evaluated by subjecting it to continuous 200 cycles of electrocatalytic activity, Fig. 6(b). A negligible change in electrocatalytic activity was observed even for continuous exposure to electrocatalytic cycles. Unlike adsorbed/drop casted carbon films, our method provides a stable and uniform coating of the composite structure, as shown in the inset of Fig. 6(b). The cyclic electrocatalytic stability of the developed carbon electrode (Fig. 6(b)) was higher than the conventional Pt electrode (Fig. 6(c)). Apart from the electrocatalytic stability, the stability of the electrode was tested in the electrolyte and water, as shown in Fig. S6(a). Similar transmittance of the solvents before and after immersion of electrode confirms that the carbon materials are not dissolved in the solvents. A slightly lower transmittance in the electrolyte sample was observed, which is due to the adsorption of electrolyte ions on the carbon surface. Fig. S6(b) validates that there are no peeled-off chunks of the coating in the solvent, hence the proposed structure can bear harsh chemical environments. The comparatively higher corrosion resistance of the carbon structures imparts stability to carbon electrodes whereas the Pt readily undergoes the degradation in a harsh electrolyte environment. The superior cyclic electrocatalytic stability of in-situ grown NCQDs is due to firm attachment with MWCNTs, by a strong covalent bonding; hence, NCQDs were not peeled off in the electrolyte solution. Owing to strong mutual bonding, our suggested heterostructure is preferable in contrast to the adsorbed NCQDs and MWCNTs heterostructure, which are connected by weak hydrogen bonding [19]. Upon continuous exposure to solvents, detachment of quantum dots is possible in such an adsorbed composite structure, as developed previously [19]. The convincing electrocatalytic stability of the NCQDs@MWCNT proves that our strategy provides a firm overlapped interface to the microwave-assisted grown NCQDs.

To analyze the effect of NCQDs growth on charge flow dynamics in the carbon heterostructure, we performed electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The EIS analysis is an effective approach to understand the enhanced charge transfer reactions at the electrolyte and catalyst interface. To elucidate the resistance contributed by the counter electrodes, we performed EIS analysis using symmetrical cell configuration, with two similar face to face sandwiched electrodes. Indeed, the symmetrical cell resembles the DSSC structure, however, photoanode (dye covered TiO2) is omitted, so that the resistance contributed by the photoanode can be neglected. The intersection of the semicircular EIS curve at the x-axis is referred to as series resistance (Rs), while the intensity along the y-axis reflects the charge transfer resistance (RCT) [45]. Form the EIS curve, both of these internal resistances can be calculated by using the Randles-type cell model, as given in the inset of Fig. S7(a) [46]. The lower resistance of the electrodes is concordant with the reduced internal resistance of DSSC, as a result of which an enhanced fill factor (FF) and power conversion efficiency are observed.

According to the EIS results given in Fig. 7 (a), the RCT of the NCQDs grown MWCNTs is decreased by fiftyfold. Bare MWCNTs exhibited RCT of 41.7 Ω, which was decreased to 0.78 Ω by the NCQDs growth, as compared in the inset of Fig. 7(a). Furthermore, the RCT of the composite electrode is much smaller than the Pt electrode (RCT = 16.15 Ω), as shown in Fig. S7(a). As evident from the x-axis intersects of all EIS curves, the RS of all electrodes was almost similar. RS mainly depends on the conductivity of the FTO glass substrate, which is same for all of the samples [47]. The bare MWCNTs showed only two semicircles, however, the NCQDs@MWCNT curve can be divided into three sections, as highlighted by a dotted line in Fig. 7(a). The first semicircle in the medium frequency range is formed by the contribution of both double-layer capacitance and RCT of the electrode/electrolyte interface [48]. The second semicircle is associated with the diffusion-controlled Nernstian element, while the third incomplete semicircle is due to the internal resistance of the electrolyte [46]. Such a multistep electron transfer mechanism is commonly observed in porous and functional carbon electrodes, due to fast electrolyte diffusion and highly active sites [46]. Contrarily, high aggregation and lower conductivity of the bare MWCNTs cause fusion of the first two semi-circles in the EIS curve [48].

The drastically lowered impedances of NCQDs anchored MWCNTs were due to additional active sites and a high degree of dispersion imparted by the NCQDs. Mainly, an extremely depressed first semicircle of NCQDs@MWCNTs indicates a remarkably low initial charge transfer barrier, required to initiate the electrochemical reaction. In addition, the dramatically low Nernst diffusion is imparted by defect rich morphology of NCQDs, which provides excessive channels for the diffusion of electrolyte ions [49]. High-speed electrolyte infiltration through a thoroughly dispersed NCQDs@MWCNT network also plays a key role in lowering the diffusion impedance. Moreover, the functional surface of the grown NCQDs generates unobstructed channels for ultra-fast electrolyte transport by strong capillary action; along with realizing accessibility of the enhanced surface area [41]. In general, defect rich carbon morphologies have low charge transfer resistance; however, the scalability of such systems is limited due to long processing times and specialized equipment usage. Compared to existing systems, extraordinary porosity and defects were coupled in the MWCNT nano-networks by employing a facile technique.

The promising electrocatalytic activity and low RCT of the NCQDs grown composite structure is suitable for the development of Pt-free DSSC. Therefore, the developed composite was coated on FTO glass, and its ability to serve as a counter electrode in DSSC was examined. The photovoltaic response curves Fig. 7(b) shows a considerably improved performance achieved by NCQDs modified MWCNT structure. Compared to bare MWCNT, the power conversion efficiency (η) of the NCQDs@MWCNT was 50% higher. The photovoltaic performance of the composite electrode is comparable to the Pt electrode (Fig. S7(b)). Photovoltaic parameters of the DSSC fabricated by both of the MWCNT electrodes are given in Table 2 . The improved photovoltaic performance of the composite structure is primarily because of the 40% higher FF whereas the enhanced VOC (~7%) played a minor role in improving efficiency. The JSC of all samples remained unchanged, because of the same photoanode structure used. Owing to similar JSC, the external quantum efficiency (EQE) of Pt and NCQDs@MWCNT electrode was recorded to be similar, as shown in Fig. S7(c). Most importantly, the reduced internal impedance of the NCQDs modified MWCNTs reflects a 40% higher FF. The lower value of RCT reduces the internal impedance of the solar cell, hence improving the overall FF of the solar cell. The proposed modification of the counter electrode accelerates the charge transfer reaction, hence lowering the internal resistance of the solar cell. A similar increase in FF was observed by others, due to the lower RSUM (combined effect of RCT, RS, and capacitive impedance) [50]. Other parameters such as surface charge redistribution, higher dispersion, and enhanced surface area by NCQDs growth further assist in the enhanced electrocatalytic activity and solar cell performance.

To probe the reason for the enhancement in the performance of DSSC by NCQDs inclusions, we analyzed the surface and electronic structure of carbon heterostructures. The difference in electronic structure imparts the difference in electrocatalytic performance. For the purpose, the light absorbance was analyzed using a UV–Visible spectrophotometer, as shown in Fig. 8 (a). Bare MWCNTs showed a broad background absorbance spectrum, with a characteristic high energy absorption peak around 250 nm. In contrast, NCQDs@MWCNT showed additional shouldered peaks, which are formed due to NCQDs absorbance. The NCQDs absorbance spectra can be divided into three main sections; π-π*, n- π*, and surface states related absorbance, as highlighted in Fig. 8(a) [39,51–54]. The π-π* transitions in MWCNTs and NCQDs are originated from sp2 hybridized clusters of carbon. Whereas, other low energy transitions by visible light are interpreted on the basis of CN/CO related surface states, which increase the probability of excitation at lower energies [38]. Such energy states reduce the bandgap of bare MWCNTs by the strong electronic interactions of grown NCQDs. The mutual electronic interactions and charge diffusion between NCQDs and MWCNTs were confirmed from the fluorescence quenching of NCQDs@MWCNTs solution. A bright color emission of NCQDs can be observed in NCQDs colloid under 365 nm light due to the quantum confinement effect, as shown in the inset of Fig. 8(a) [40]. Such a characteristic emission of NCQDs is quenched after NCQDs growth on MWCNTs, as shown in Fig. 8(a). The mutual electronic interaction between MWCNTs and NCQDs impedes the direct recombination of electron-hole pair, which turns off the fluorescence in the composite structure.

The mutual electronic interactions and structural defects were further investigated with Raman analysis to check the charge transport behavior in modified MWCNTs, as shown in Fig. 8(b). Mainly the D band and G bands were observed, which reflects the proportion of disordered carbon and crystalline carbon in the synthesized heterostructure, respectively. Importantly, a significant intensity of surface defects peak centered at 1341 cm−1, which appears due to the defect rich surface of NCQDs. Incorporation of surface defects in the smooth sp2 carbon clusters plays a critical role in low potential charge transfer, as a multi-edge defect surface serves as an active site for charge transfer and storage reactions [55]. Additionally, a considerably strong G band peak centered at 1574 cm−1 reflects the presence of a noteworthy concentration of conjugated crystalline carbon content, available for charge conductivity. The shift of d-band to a lower frequency in Raman spectroscopy is related to variation in carrier density, as shown in Fig. 8(b). The D band is formed by the double resonant process, hence carrier concentration is strongly related to the D band shift. NCQDs growth caused the D band softening (shift to a lower frequency), which proves the higher density of electrons near the Fermi level [56]. In addition, the G band also showed a shift to a lower frequency, which is due to higher nonadiabatic Kohn anomalies at G-point. NCQDs growth caused the sharpening of the G band, which is related to an increased barrier of decay channels of phonons to excitons [57]. From the Raman shift, it is evident that NCQDs growth favors the electron transport behavior in MWCNT.

The variation in the electronic band structure of MWCNTs was further analyzed by approximating the HOMO (highest occupied molecular orbital) and LUMO (lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) using cyclic voltammetry. Ferrocene was used as an external reference for the calibration of MWCNT's band structure. The cyclic voltammogram of Ferrocene is given in Fig. S8, showing the characteristic oxidation and reduction peaks at a positive and negative potential, respectively. The oxidation and reduction potential is directly associated with the HOMO and LUMO band of the material. Under the same experimental conditions, bare MWCNTs showed higher EPP as compared to NCQDs grown MWCNTs, as shown in Fig. 8(c). From the onset potential of both carbon structures, we calculated the HOMO and LUMO energy levels by using the Eq. (1).

The electrochemical bandgap of bare MWCNT and NCQDs@MWCNTs was calculated to be 0.91 eV and 0.41 eV, respectively. The additional oxygenated and nitrogenated surface states are formed by the NCQDs growth, which narrows down the bandgap of NCQDs@MWCNT, as shown in Fig. 8(d).

The performance of the proposed composite structure is comparable to the metallic and metal-chalcogenides based counter electrodes, as compared in Table S1. It is important to note that, to get the few grams of metal powders, tons of soil are processed; hence, the sustainability of metallic catalysts is questionable. In contrast, NCQDs are synthesized from an organic source of citric acid and urea [58,59,60]. Conclusively, our proposed rapid strategy for atomic-scale modulation and structure design of MWCNTs based metal-free catalyst systems, with promising light-harvesting capabilities. The combined features of conductivity and defects were coupled in a single composite structure by our proposed ultrafast and facile synthetic strategy. On the other hand, previous methods require strong irradiation, high-temperature annealing, and harsh chemical treatments to generate essentially required defects, which require a long time, high temperature, and specialized equipment [8,9]. The proposed diverse method can be extended for modification of other catalysts: specifically, 2D materials, as grown NCQDs can serve as spacer material for inhibition of restacking.

NCQDs were successfully grown on the MWCNT, using microwave-assisted ultrafast carbonization (i.e. 10 min) of citric acid and urea. NCQDs growth integrates a high conductivity and defects rich morphology in a single heterostructure, hence electrocatalytic activity was doubled. Superb surface functionality of the grown NCQDs promotes the dispersion of MWCNTs, thus forming unobstructed channels for electrolyte infiltration, thus reducing the RCT by 50 times. Moreover, auxochromic nature of doped nitrogen in the core of NCQDs increases the density of electrons near the Fermi level, thus realizing the charge transfer at a lower potential. The highly doped surface of NCQDs is highly reactive and has exceptional electrolyte binding and reducing capability. Conclusively, facile NCQDs growth improved the surface reactivity and internal band structure, which in turn reflected 50% enhanced photovoltaic performance (η = 9.28%) compared to bare MWCNT electrode (η = 6.17%). In contrast to other surface tuning methods, the potential of our proposed strategy lies in its facile and scalable production.
